report_metadata.cancer_type: Polymorphous Adenocarcinoma of the Hard Palate
report_metadata.clinical_context: Polymorphous adenocarcinoma (PAC) of the hard palate in a 45-year-old male. T1N0M0, low-grade, PRKD1 mutation positive. This is a low-grade salivary gland malignancy with excellent prognosis. Wide excision alone is curative for T1N0 disease with negative margins. Adjuvant radiation therapy is rarely needed for T1 tumors and should be avoided to prevent overtreatment. The PRKD1 E710D hotspot mutation is diagnostic and confirms indolent biology. Perineural invasion is histologically common (60-75%) but does not change the favorable prognosis in low-grade PAC. Palatal obturator prosthesis may be needed if wide excision creates an oroantral communication. 5-year disease-specific survival exceeds 98%. 10-year DSS is 94%. Recurrence rate is 9-19% but typically manageable with re-excision. Lymph node metastasis occurs in only 9-17% of all PAC cases and is essentially absent in T1N0 disease. Chemotherapy has no role in low-grade PAC. The key clinical principle is to avoid overtreatment of this indolent malignancy.

treatments.name: Wide Local Excision / Partial Palatectomy with Negative Margins

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO 2021 and ESMO/EURACAN 2022 guidelines both recommend surgical excision as primary treatment for salivary gland malignancies. Multiple large retrospective series (>500 patients combined) confirm excellent outcomes with surgery alone for low-grade PAC. No RCTs exist because cure rates are so high. NCCN Head and Neck guidelines endorse surgery for resectable salivary gland cancers.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year disease-specific survival 98%, 10-year DSS 94%. For T1N0 low-grade PAC with negative margins, cure rates approach 95-100%. This is a curative procedure. The 45-year-old patient has essentially normal life expectancy after complete excision with clear margins.

accessibility.rationale: Wide excision or partial palatectomy is a standard oral/maxillofacial surgery procedure available at any head and neck surgery center worldwide. No specialized equipment required. Straightforward outpatient or short-stay procedure.

safety_profile.rationale: Minimal surgical morbidity for T1 palatal lesions. May create oroantral communication requiring palatal obturator. Risks include wound infection, bleeding, minor speech/swallowing changes. No systemic toxicity. Long-term quality of life is excellent with prosthetic rehabilitation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universally applicable to all PAC regardless of molecular profile. PRKD1 mutation status confirms the diagnosis and favorable biology but does not alter surgical recommendation. All T1N0 PAC patients are surgical candidates.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision of the primary tumor with adequate margins of uninvolved tissue (typically 1-1.5 cm). For hard palate tumors, this involves partial palatectomy with resection through mucoperiosteum and underlying palatal bone if involved. Frozen section margin analysis ensures negative margins intraoperatively. The greater palatine nerve is traced and resected with frozen section control given the propensity for perineural invasion in PAC.

key_evidence.study_name: National Cancer Database analysis of polymorphous adenocarcinoma outcomes

biomarker_requirements: Histologic confirmation of PAC (PRKD1 mutation supports diagnosis)
No specific biomarker required for surgical eligibility
notable_side_effects: Mild speech changes (temporary, improves with obturator)
treatments.availability: Universally available — standard surgical procedure at all head and neck surgery centers

treatments.name: Palatal Obturator Prosthesis — Post-Excision Rehabilitation

evidence_level.rationale: Decades of prosthodontic literature supporting obturator prostheses for maxillary/palatal defects. ASCO and ESMO guidelines emphasize functional rehabilitation after palatal surgery. Systematic reviews confirm effectiveness in restoring speech, swallowing, and cosmesis.

survival_benefit.rationale: Does not directly improve survival but is essential for quality of life and functional rehabilitation after palatectomy. Enables adequate nutrition and prevents aspiration. Indirectly supports overall health and recovery.

accessibility.rationale: Available through maxillofacial prosthodontists at major dental and surgical centers. Three-phase approach: surgical obturator (immediate), temporary obturator (healing phase), definitive obturator (after complete healing). Custom-fabricated for each patient.

safety_profile.rationale: Non-invasive prosthetic device. No systemic side effects. Minimal complications — occasional fit adjustments needed. No interference with oncologic surveillance.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universally applicable to all patients with palatal defects after surgical excision regardless of tumor biology or molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: A removable prosthesis that closes the surgical defect in the hard palate, separating the oral cavity from the nasal cavity/maxillary sinus. Restores the palatal seal necessary for normal speech (particularly velopharyngeal closure), deglutition, and mastication. Three sequential phases: surgical obturator (placed at surgery), temporary/interim obturator (during healing, 3-4 months), and definitive obturator (after complete healing and tissue maturation).

key_evidence.study_name: Systematic review of definitive palatal obturator applications

biomarker_requirements: Not biomarker-dependent
Required only if surgical defect creates oroantral communication
notable_side_effects: Adaptation period for speech and swallowing
treatments.availability: Available at maxillofacial prosthodontic centers — standard rehabilitative care

treatments.name: Active Surveillance Without Adjuvant Therapy (Post-Complete Excision)

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO 2021 guidelines and NCCN Head and Neck guidelines support observation without adjuvant therapy for completely excised T1N0 low-grade salivary gland cancers with negative margins. National Cancer Database shows 74.7% of PAC patients received surgery alone without adjuvant RT.

survival_benefit.rationale: Avoiding unnecessary adjuvant RT preserves quality of life without compromising oncologic outcomes. 5-year DSS 98% with surgery alone for low-grade PAC. No evidence that adjuvant RT improves outcomes in T1N0 with negative margins. Long-term surveillance catches the 9-19% recurrence rate early for re-excision.

accessibility.rationale: Clinical follow-up with physical examination and imaging is universally available. No specialized treatment required. Reduces healthcare costs and patient burden compared to unnecessary adjuvant therapy.

safety_profile.rationale: No treatment-related toxicity. Avoids radiation-induced xerostomia, mucositis, osteoradionecrosis, and second malignancy risk that would accompany unnecessary adjuvant RT. Preserves quality of life in a young patient.

biomarker_match.rationale: Appropriate for all T1N0 low-grade PAC with negative margins. PRKD1 mutation positivity confirms indolent biology supporting conservative management.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Structured clinical surveillance program after complete surgical excision with negative margins. Includes regular clinical examination of the oral cavity and neck (every 3-4 months for years 1-2, every 6 months for years 3-5, then annually), periodic imaging as indicated, and patient education on self-examination. The goal is early detection of local recurrence (9-19% risk) while avoiding the toxicity of unnecessary adjuvant therapy.

key_evidence.study_name: Role of elective neck dissection and adjuvant radiation therapy in patients with polymorphous adenocarcinoma (NCDB analysis)

biomarker_requirements: Negative surgical margins confirmed on final pathology
Low-grade histology
No lymphovascular invasion
No high-grade transformation
notable_side_effects: Risk of late recurrence (may occur >10 years after initial surgery)

treatments.name: Adjuvant Radiation Therapy (IMRT/Proton) — For High-Risk Features Only

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO and ESMO guidelines recommend adjuvant RT selectively for salivary gland cancers with adverse features (positive margins, perineural invasion, high-grade transformation, T3-T4 tumors). For T1N0 low-grade PAC with negative margins, adjuvant RT is NOT recommended. Only 18% of PAC patients in the NCDB received adjuvant RT.

survival_benefit.rationale: No proven survival benefit for T1N0 low-grade PAC with negative margins. Adjuvant RT reduces local recurrence in high-risk scenarios (positive margins, close margins, perineural invasion with high-grade features) but adds toxicity without clear OS benefit in this specific clinical scenario.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available. Proton therapy available at specialized centers with potential benefit for reduced late toxicity in young patients. Standard radiation oncology infrastructure.

safety_profile.rationale: Radiation to the palate/oral cavity causes mucositis, xerostomia, taste changes, dental complications (radiation caries, osteoradionecrosis risk), and long-term dysphagia. These toxicities are disproportionate for a T1N0 low-grade tumor with excellent surgical cure rates. Risk of radiation-induced second malignancy in a 45-year-old with long life expectancy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-driven. Indication is based on pathologic risk features, not molecular profile. For this specific T1N0 PRKD1-mutant case, RT is generally NOT indicated.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-energy photon (IMRT) or proton beams deliver targeted radiation to the tumor bed and surgical margins to eradicate residual microscopic disease. IMRT conforms dose to target while sparing adjacent salivary glands and critical structures. Proton therapy offers superior dose conformity with reduced integral dose to surrounding normal tissues. Typical dose: 60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions over 6 weeks.

key_evidence.study_name: ASCO Guideline: Management of Salivary Gland Malignancy

biomarker_requirements: NOT recommended for T1N0 low-grade PAC with negative margins
Consider only if: positive margins, close margins (<1mm), high-grade transformation, or extensive perineural invasion
treatments.availability: Widely available — IMRT at all radiation centers; proton therapy at specialized centers

treatments.name: Re-Excision for Positive or Close Margins

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO and NCCN guidelines recommend re-excision when feasible for positive margins rather than defaulting to adjuvant RT. Standard surgical oncology principle. Multiple retrospective series confirm improved outcomes with negative margins.

survival_benefit.rationale: Achieving negative margins is the most important prognostic factor for local control. Positive margins are associated with significantly decreased survival in minor salivary gland cancers. Re-excision to achieve clear margins can restore the excellent prognosis of completely excised PAC.

accessibility.rationale: Standard surgical procedure. Available at any head and neck surgical center. Less complex than the initial excision as the anatomy is already defined.

safety_profile.rationale: Second surgical procedure with associated anesthetic and wound healing risks. May enlarge the palatal defect requiring more extensive prosthetic rehabilitation. However, avoids the systemic toxicity of adjuvant RT.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. Indicated for any PAC with positive surgical margins regardless of molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Surgical re-excision of the tumor bed with wider margins to achieve histologically negative margins. Frozen section analysis guides the extent of re-excision. Addresses residual microscopic disease at the margin while avoiding the toxicity of adjuvant radiation therapy.

key_evidence.study_name: Minor salivary gland tumors of the palate: clinical and pathologic correlates of outcome

biomarker_requirements: Positive or close (<1mm) surgical margins on initial excision pathology
notable_side_effects: Larger palatal defect requiring more complex obturator

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (If Adjuvant RT Indicated)

evidence_level.rationale: MSK-led clinical trial (2025 ASTRO) demonstrated proton therapy achieves similar oncologic outcomes with less toxicity than photon IMRT for head and neck cancers, including 60 patients with parotid/salivary gland tumors. Johns Hopkins case studies demonstrate benefit for salivary gland tumors.

survival_benefit.rationale: Equivalent oncologic outcomes to IMRT. The advantage is reduced toxicity, not improved survival. Grade 2+ mucositis: 7.5% (proton) vs 22.2% (IMRT). For a 45-year-old with long life expectancy, reduced late toxicity is clinically meaningful.

accessibility.rationale: Limited to specialized proton therapy centers. Fewer than 50 centers in the US. Higher cost. Insurance coverage may require pre-authorization and clinical justification. Only relevant if adjuvant RT is indicated (which is unlikely for T1N0 low-grade PAC).

safety_profile.rationale: Superior safety profile compared to photon IMRT. Reduced integral dose to normal tissues results in less xerostomia, less mucositis, and lower second malignancy risk. Particularly advantageous in young patients with long life expectancy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-driven. Indicated based on anatomic and dosimetric advantages for palatal/maxillary tumors, not molecular profile. For this patient, only relevant if high-risk features necessitate adjuvant RT.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beams deposit energy via the Bragg peak, delivering maximum dose at tumor depth with minimal exit dose beyond the target. This results in significantly reduced radiation exposure to surrounding normal tissues compared to photon IMRT. For palatal tumors, this translates to better sparing of contralateral salivary glands, brainstem, optic structures, and temporal lobes.

key_evidence.study_name: MSK Phase 2 trial: Proton vs Photon for Head and Neck Cancer

biomarker_requirements: Not biomarker-dependent
Only relevant if adjuvant RT is clinically indicated
notable_side_effects: Mucositis (reduced compared to IMRT: 7.5% vs 22.2% grade 2+)
treatments.availability: Limited to specialized proton therapy centers — fewer than 50 in the US

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (Anti-PD-1 Immunotherapy) — For Recurrent/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-028 Phase 1b showed activity in recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancers. PD-L1 expression predicts response. No data specific to PAC. Most salivary gland immunotherapy data is in adenoid cystic carcinoma and salivary duct carcinoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Modest benefit in R/M salivary gland cancers. ORR ~12% in KEYNOTE-028. Median PFS approximately 4 months. Not appropriate for this patient's T1N0 curable disease. Only relevant in the unlikely scenario of recurrent/metastatic disease refractory to surgery and RT.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for multiple indications including MSI-H/TMB-H tumors. Available at most oncology centers. Off-label for salivary gland cancers. Insurance coverage variable for off-label use.

safety_profile.rationale: Immune-related adverse events: thyroiditis, colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, dermatitis. Generally manageable but can be serious. Completely inappropriate for curable T1N0 PAC — toxicity is disproportionate to disease risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 expression status unknown for this patient. Low-grade PAC typically has low immune cell infiltration. Response in salivary gland cancers correlates with PD-L1 CPS. MSI-H/TMB-H status would expand eligibility.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1 receptor on T cells, restoring anti-tumor immune surveillance. By preventing the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, activated T cells can recognize and destroy tumor cells expressing tumor-associated antigens.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-028: Pembrolizumab for Advanced Salivary Gland Carcinoma

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS >=1 (for salivary gland cancer indication)
Only for recurrent/metastatic disease unresponsive to surgery and RT
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for multiple indications; off-label for salivary gland cancer

treatments.name: Lenvatinib (Anti-VEGFR/FGFR TKI) — For Recurrent/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data in recurrent/metastatic adenoid cystic carcinoma: ORR 15.6%, disease control rate 90.6%, median PFS 17.5 months. 2025 ASCO data on lenvatinib + pembrolizumab combination. No specific data in PAC.

survival_benefit.rationale: Primarily disease stabilization rather than regression in salivary gland cancers. Median PFS 17.5 months in ACC. Not appropriate for curable T1N0 disease. Only relevant for distant recurrence/metastasis.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for thyroid cancer and other indications. Oral formulation. Available at most oncology centers. Off-label for salivary gland cancers.

safety_profile.rationale: Hypertension, diarrhea, fatigue, proteinuria, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, weight loss, hypothyroidism. Significant toxicity burden that is inappropriate for curable disease.

biomarker_match.rationale: Multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, RET, KIT, PDGFRa. Not specific to PRKD1-mutant tumors. Mechanism is anti-angiogenic rather than targeting the driver mutation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, RET, KIT, and PDGFRalpha. Its anti-angiogenic and anti-proliferative effects reduce tumor blood supply and inhibit growth factor signaling. In salivary gland cancers, the anti-angiogenic mechanism provides disease stabilization.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib in Progressive Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma

biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker required
Only for recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancer after failure of surgery and RT
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for thyroid cancer; off-label for salivary gland cancers

treatments.name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab Combination — For Recurrent/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial presented at 2025 ASCO (NCT active). Results emerging. Combination of anti-VEGFR TKI + anti-PD-1. Rationale is that lenvatinib may enhance immune checkpoint inhibitor efficacy by modulating the tumor microenvironment.

survival_benefit.rationale: Early data emerging from 2025 ASCO presentation. Lenvatinib alone showed median PFS 17.5 months in ACC. Combination may offer incremental benefit but prior combination data did not exceed monotherapy. Completely inappropriate for curable T1N0 disease.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents individually FDA-approved but combination is off-label for salivary gland cancer. Available through clinical trials or compassionate use. High combined cost.

safety_profile.rationale: Additive toxicity from both agents. Hypertension, immune-related adverse events, fatigue, diarrhea. Combination toxicity profile is more burdensome than either agent alone. Grossly disproportionate for curable T1N0 PAC.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not specific to PRKD1-mutant PAC. Targeting VEGFR/FGFR pathways and PD-1 axis. May have activity in salivary gland cancers but not tailored to the specific driver mutation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combination of lenvatinib (multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR/FGFR pathways) with pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor). Lenvatinib modulates the tumor immune microenvironment by reducing immunosuppressive cells (regulatory T cells, tumor-associated macrophages) and increasing CD8+ T cell infiltration, potentially enhancing the efficacy of anti-PD-1 therapy.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II study of lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab in R/M salivary gland cancers

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 status may predict immunotherapy component response
Only for recurrent/metastatic disease refractory to standard approaches
treatments.availability: Available through clinical trials; both agents individually FDA-approved for other indications

treatments.name: CRT0066101 (Pan-PKD Inhibitor) — Preclinical / Experimental

evidence_level.rationale: Preclinical data only. CRT0066101 is a selective pan-PKD inhibitor with IC50 of 1 nM for PKD1. Demonstrated efficacy in breast, pancreatic, bladder cancer xenografts. No clinical trials in salivary gland cancer or PAC specifically. Theoretical rationale based on PRKD1 being the driver mutation in PAC.

survival_benefit.rationale: No human clinical data. Preclinical tumor growth inhibition in xenograft models of other cancers. Unknown whether targeting PRKD1 in an already low-grade, indolent tumor would provide meaningful benefit.

accessibility.rationale: Research compound only. Not FDA-approved for any indication. No active clinical trials in salivary gland cancer. Available only as a laboratory reagent. Years from potential clinical development.

safety_profile.rationale: Well tolerated in preclinical mouse models with no significant side effects. Oral bioavailability is favorable. However, no human safety data exists.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets the PRKD1 kinase domain where the E710D activating mutation resides. PAC with PRKD1 mutation would be the ideal biomarker-selected population for this agent. Highest biomarker specificity of any treatment option.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: CRT0066101 is a pan-protein kinase D (PKD) inhibitor that potently inhibits PKD1, PKD2, and PKD3 with IC50 values of 1, 2.5, and 2 nM respectively. In PRKD1-mutant PAC, the E710D hotspot mutation constitutively activates PRKD1 kinase activity. CRT0066101 directly inhibits this aberrant kinase signaling, blocking downstream NF-kB activation, cell proliferation, and survival pathways. The compound inhibits phosphorylation of MYC, MAPK1/3, AKT, and YAP.

key_evidence.study_name: Small-Molecule Inhibitor Targeting Protein Kinase D: A Potential Therapeutic Strategy (Review)

biomarker_requirements: PRKD1 E710D activating mutation (present in 73% of PAC)
Ideally confirmed kinase activation
notable_side_effects: No significant side effects in preclinical models
Potential for on-target effects on normal PKD signaling
treatments.availability: Research compound only — not available for clinical use

treatments.name: Cisplatin + Docetaxel Chemotherapy — For Unresectable/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data in recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancers. ORR 46.5%, median PFS 7.2 months. However, low-grade PAC is generally chemoresistant. Most chemotherapy data is in high-grade salivary duct carcinoma and adenoid cystic carcinoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Modest response rates with short PFS. Low-grade salivary gland cancers respond poorly to chemotherapy. Not appropriate for curable T1N0 disease. Only considered for distant metastatic disease refractory to other approaches.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents widely available and FDA-approved. Standard chemotherapy regimen administered at any oncology center. Well-established dosing and monitoring protocols.

safety_profile.rationale: Cisplatin: nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, neuropathy, nausea/vomiting, myelosuppression. Docetaxel: neutropenia, alopecia, fluid retention, neuropathy. Combined toxicity is significant and completely disproportionate for curable low-grade disease.

biomarker_match.rationale: Non-targeted cytotoxic therapy. Not specific to PRKD1-mutant PAC. Low-grade histology predicts poor response to cytotoxic chemotherapy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms platinum-DNA crosslinks causing DNA damage and apoptosis. Docetaxel stabilizes microtubules preventing mitotic spindle disassembly, causing cell cycle arrest at G2/M. The combination provides synergistic cytotoxicity through complementary mechanisms of DNA damage and mitotic inhibition.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Cisplatin Plus Weekly Docetaxel in Metastatic/Recurrent Salivary Gland Cancer

biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker required
Adequate renal function for cisplatin
Only for metastatic/unresectable disease — NOT for T1N0 curable PAC

treatments.name: Vorinostat + Pembrolizumab Combination — For Recurrent/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data in recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancers: 25 patients, median PFS 6.9 months, median OS 14 months. Combination of HDAC inhibitor + anti-PD-1. Promising early results but small sample size.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 6.9 months, median OS 14 months in R/M salivary gland cancers. Modest but meaningful in the salvage setting. Not applicable to curable T1N0 disease.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved individually but combination is investigational for salivary gland cancer. Available through clinical trials. Off-label use possible but insurance coverage uncertain.

safety_profile.rationale: Additive toxicity: HDAC inhibitor side effects (thrombocytopenia, fatigue, diarrhea, nausea) plus immune-related adverse events from pembrolizumab. Disproportionate for curable disease.

biomarker_match.rationale: Vorinostat modulates epigenetic landscape and may enhance anti-tumor immunity. Not specific to PRKD1 mutation. Rationale is epigenetic priming of immune response rather than targeting the driver mutation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vorinostat (HDAC inhibitor) modifies chromatin accessibility and gene expression, upregulating tumor antigen presentation and PD-L1 expression on tumor cells. This epigenetic priming enhances the anti-tumor immune response induced by pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1). The combination aims to convert immunologically cold tumors into hot tumors responsive to checkpoint inhibition.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 2 study of vorinostat + pembrolizumab in recurrent metastatic salivary gland cancer

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 status may predict response
Only for recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancer
treatments.availability: Available through clinical trials; both agents individually FDA-approved

treatments.name: Hedgehog Pathway Inhibitors (Vismodegib/Sonidegib) — Preclinical Rationale

evidence_level.rationale: 2025 study (De Santana et al., Journal of Oral Pathology & Medicine) demonstrated hedgehog pathway components are present in PAC regardless of PRKD1 mutation status. Preclinical rationale only. No clinical trials of Hedgehog inhibitors in PAC.

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical data. Preclinical observation of pathway activation. Unknown whether Hedgehog inhibition would provide therapeutic benefit in an already indolent tumor.

accessibility.rationale: Vismodegib and sonidegib are FDA-approved for basal cell carcinoma. Could be used off-label but no clinical justification exists for PAC. Oral formulation.

safety_profile.rationale: Muscle spasms, alopecia, dysgeusia, weight loss, fatigue, nausea. Teratogenic. Chronic toxicity limits long-term use. Unnecessary for curable T1N0 disease.

biomarker_match.rationale: Hedgehog pathway components are present in PAC tumors. However, it is unclear whether this represents a therapeutic target or merely a developmental pathway feature of minor salivary gland differentiation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vismodegib and sonidegib inhibit the Hedgehog signaling pathway by binding to Smoothened (SMO), a transmembrane receptor. This blocks downstream GLI transcription factor activation, inhibiting cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival pathways. In PAC, hedgehog components (SHH, PTCH1, SMO, GLI1) are expressed, suggesting potential pathway dependence.

key_evidence.study_name: Hedgehog Components Are Present in Polymorphous Adenocarcinoma of the Salivary Gland Regardless of PRKD1 Mutation and Tissue Invasion

biomarker_requirements: Hedgehog pathway activation (SHH, PTCH1, SMO, GLI1 expression)
Confirmed PAC histology
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for basal cell carcinoma; no clinical data supporting use in PAC

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib Plus Pembrolizumab in Patients with Recurrent/Metastatic Salivary Gland Cancers
biomarker_requirements: Recurrent or metastatic salivary gland cancer
All histologic subtypes including PAC
clinical_trials.early_results: Results presented at ASCO 2025 Annual Meeting. Combination of anti-VEGFR TKI with anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor.

clinical_trials.title: Testing Ado-Trastuzumab Emtansine as Treatment for HER2-Positive Salivary Gland Cancer
sites: UCSF and other US academic centers
biomarker_requirements: HER2-positive salivary gland cancer
Not applicable to PRKD1-mutant PAC unless HER2 co-expression confirmed
clinical_trials.early_results: Ongoing enrollment. Comparing T-DM1 to standard chemotherapy in HER2+ salivary gland cancers.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Trial of Darolutamide and Leuprolide Acetate in Androgen Receptor Positive Salivary Gland Cancer
biomarker_requirements: Androgen receptor positive salivary gland cancer
Recurrent/metastatic or unresectable disease
clinical_trials.early_results: Ongoing. Androgen receptor targeting in salivary duct carcinoma primarily; unlikely to benefit PAC patients.

clinical_trials.title: Multicenter Phase II Trial of Goserelin Acetate with Pembrolizumab in Salivary Gland Cancer
biomarker_requirements: Recurrent/metastatic salivary gland cancer
Various histologic subtypes
clinical_trials.early_results: Combining androgen deprivation with checkpoint inhibition. Results expected 2025.

clinical_trials.trial_id: NCT (Proton vs Photon in H&N Cancer — MSK)
clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Trial Comparing Proton Beam to Photon IMRT in Head and Neck Cancers (Including Salivary Gland)
sites: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, New York
biomarker_requirements: Head and neck cancer requiring adjuvant or definitive RT
60/98 patients had parotid/salivary gland tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: Proton therapy resulted in similar oncologic outcomes with significantly less acute toxicity. Grade 2+ mucositis: 7.5% (proton) vs 22.2% (IMRT). Quality of life improved with proton therapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Complete surgical excision removes the tumor while the palatal obturator restores the functional defect. This is the essential treatment combination for hard palate PAC. Excision cures the cancer; the obturator restores speech, swallowing, and cosmesis. The three-phase obturator approach (surgical, interim, definitive) optimizes functional recovery.

For T1N0 low-grade PAC with negative margins, surgery alone followed by structured surveillance is the recommended approach. Adjuvant RT is NOT indicated. 74.7% of PAC patients in the NCDB received surgery alone. Adding RT would represent overtreatment with unnecessary toxicity. Surveillance catches the 9-19% recurrence rate early for re-excision.

If initial excision yields positive or close margins, re-excision is preferred over adjuvant RT when anatomically feasible. If re-excision is not possible, adjuvant RT (preferably proton therapy for reduced late toxicity in this young patient) is indicated to address residual microscopic disease.

Only for recurrent/metastatic PAC (unlikely scenario for T1N0). Lenvatinib modulates tumor immune microenvironment, potentially enhancing checkpoint inhibitor efficacy. Anti-angiogenic effects complement immune-mediated anti-tumor activity. Phase 2 data presented at ASCO 2025.

supportive_care.evidence: Iowa Head and Neck Protocols, systematic reviews of palatal obturator outcomes. Three-phase approach (surgical, interim, definitive obturator) is standard of care after palatectomy. Quality of life outcomes are excellent with experienced maxillofacial prosthodontist.
supportive_care.benefit: Restores speech intelligibility, swallowing function, and cosmetic appearance after palatal excision. Prevents nasal regurgitation and aspiration. Eliminates oroantral communication. Critical for quality of life in a 45-year-old with long life expectancy.

supportive_care.evidence: ASCO guidelines recommend multidisciplinary supportive care for head and neck cancer patients. Speech-language pathology assessment pre- and post-surgery is standard of care. Therapeutic exercises improve adaptation to palatal prosthesis.
supportive_care.benefit: Accelerates functional recovery of speech and swallowing. Compensatory strategies during healing phase. Optimizes adaptation to palatal obturator. Important for social and professional functioning.

supportive_care.approach: Dental Assessment and Ongoing Dental Care
supportive_care.evidence: American Dental Association guidelines for cancer patients. Pre-operative dental assessment identifies issues that may complicate surgery or prosthetic rehabilitation. Ongoing dental care ensures obturator-supporting teeth remain healthy.
supportive_care.benefit: Preserves dentition needed to support palatal obturator prosthesis. Prevents dental complications that could compromise prosthetic rehabilitation. Important for long-term functional outcome.

supportive_care.evidence: ESPEN guidelines for nutritional support in head and neck cancer surgery. Perioperative nutritional optimization improves wound healing and reduces complications.
supportive_care.benefit: Optimizes wound healing after palatectomy. Dietary modifications during recovery period. Soft/liquid diet guidance until obturator is fitted. Weight maintenance during perioperative period.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Distress Management guidelines. Cancer diagnosis in young patients causes significant anxiety even with excellent prognosis. Counseling about the highly favorable outlook (>98% 5-year DSS) is important.
supportive_care.benefit: Addresses anxiety from cancer diagnosis. Communicates favorable prognosis clearly. Supports coping with facial/oral changes during recovery. Reduces cancer-related distress and improves adherence to surveillance schedule.

supportive_care.evidence: ASCO 2021 guidelines and NCCN Head and Neck guidelines recommend structured long-term follow-up. PAC can recur late (>10 years after initial treatment). Clinical examination every 3-4 months for years 1-2, every 6 months for years 3-5, then annually.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of local recurrence (9-19% risk). Identifies late recurrences that may occur beyond 10 years. Enables timely re-excision for recurrence. Provides ongoing reassurance.

sources.title: Management of Salivary Gland Malignancy: ASCO Guideline
Salivary gland cancer: ESMO-EURACAN Clinical Practice Guideline for diagnosis, treatment and follow-up
Polymorphous Adenocarcinoma of Salivary Glands — Systematic Review and Update
Polymorphous Low-Grade Adenocarcinoma of the Salivary Glands — A Review
PRKD1 Mutations Characterize Salivary Gland Polymorphous Low-Grade Adenocarcinoma
Hotspot activating PRKD1 somatic mutations in polymorphous low-grade adenocarcinomas of the salivary glands
Role of elective neck dissection and adjuvant radiation therapy in patients with polymorphous adenocarcinoma
Surgical Management of Minor Salivary Gland Neoplasms of the Palate
